volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 2.4.415b - Section 2.6.530)

sections.label: Section 2.4.415b
sections.original: Hem , quid hoc boni est ? eu edepol specie lepida mulierem .
sections.translation: Well now , what good news is there ? ( Aside .) Dear me , a lass of comely appearance , i ’ troth .

sections.label: Section 2.4.416
sections.original: Salve , adulescens .
sections.translation: Greeting to you , young man .

sections.label: Section 2.4.416b
sections.original: Et tu multum salveto , adulescentula .
sections.translation: And many greetings to you , young woman .

sections.label: Section 2.4.417
sections.original: Ad vos venio .
sections.translation: I ’ m come to you —

sections.label: Section 2.4.417b
sections.original: Accipiam hospitio , si mox venies ⋯
sections.translation: I ’ ll receive you with a welcome , if you come in the evening , by-and-by , just such as I could like ; for just now I ’ ve no means [*] to receive you , a damsel , thus early in the morning ⋯ But what have you to say , my smiling , pretty one .

sections.label: Section 2.4.419b
sections.original: Aha , nimium familiariter
sections.translation: Oh , you ’ re handling me too familiarly .

sections.label: Section 2.4.420b
sections.original: Pro di immortales , Veneris effigia haec quidem est .
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods ! she ’ s the very image of Venus . What joyousness there is in her eyes , and , only do see , what a skin ’ tis of the vulture ’ s tint [*] ,— rather , the eagle ’ s , indeed , I meant to say . Her breasts , too , how beautiful ; and then what expression on her lips !

sections.label: Section 2.4.424
sections.original: Non ego sum pollucta pago . potin ut me abstineas manum ?
sections.translation: I ’ m no common commodity for the whole township [*] ; can ’ t you keep your hands off me ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.425
sections.original: Non licet saltem sic placide bellam belle tangere ?
sections.translation: Won ’ t you let me touch you , gentle one , in this manner , gently and lovingly ? ⋯

sections.label: Section 2.4.426
sections.original: Otium ubi erit , tum tibi operam ludo et deliciae dabo ;
sections.translation: When I have leisure , then I ’ ll be giving my attention to toying and dalliance to please you ; for the present , prithee , do either say me Yes or No to the matter for which I was sent hither .

sections.label: Section 2.4.428
sections.original: Quid nunc vis ?
sections.translation: What now is it that you wish ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.428b
sections.original: Sapienti ornatus quid velim indicium facit .
sections.translation: To a shrewd person , my equipment would give indications of what it is I want .

sections.label: Section 2.4.429
sections.original: Meus quoque hic sapienti ornatus quid velim indicium facit .
sections.translation: To a shrewd woman , this equipment , too , of mine , would give indication of what it is I want .

sections.label: Section 2.4.430
sections.original: Haec sacerdos Veneris hinc me petere aquam iussit a vobis . †
sections.translation: The Priestess there of Venus , requested me to fetch some water from your house here . ⋯

sections.label: Section 2.4.431
sections.original: At ego basilicus sum : quem nisi oras , guttam non feres .
sections.translation: But I ’ m a lordly sort of person ; unless you entreat me , you shan ’ t have a drop . We dug this well with danger to ourselves , and with tools of iron . Not a drop can be got out of me except by means of plenty of blandishments .

sections.label: Section 2.4.434
sections.original: Cur tu aquam gravare , amabo , quam hostis hosti commodat ?
sections.translation: Prithee , why do you make so much fuss about the water — a thing that even enemy affords to enemy ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.435
sections.original: Cur tu operam gravare mihi quam civis civi commodat ?
sections.translation: Why do you make so much fuss about granting a favour to me , that citizen grants to citizen ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.436
sections.original: Immo etiam tibi , mea voluptas , quae voles faciam omnia .
sections.translation: On the contrary , my sweet one , I ’ ll even do everything for you that you wish .

sections.label: Section 2.4.437
sections.original: Eugepae , salvos sum , haec iam me suam voluptatem vocat .
sections.translation: O charming ! I am favoured ; she ’ s now calling me her sweet one . The water shall be given you , so that you mayn ’ t be coaxing me in vain . Give me the pitcher .

sections.label: Section 2.4.438b
sections.original: Cape .
sections.translation: Take it ( gives it to him ) : make haste and bring it out , there ’ s a dear .

sections.label: Section 2.4.439b
sections.original: Manta , iam hic ero , voluptas mea .—
sections.translation: Stay a moment ; I ’ ll be here this instant , my sweet one . ( Goes into the cottage .) ⋯

sections.label: Section 2.4.440
sections.original: Quid sacerdoti me dicam hic demoratam tam diu ?
sections.translation: What shall I say to the Priestess for having delayed here so long a time ? ⋯ How , even still , in my wretchedness do I tremble , when with my eyes I look upon the sea . ( She looks towards the shore .)

sections.label: Section 2.4.442_450
sections.original: sed quid ego misera video procul in litore ?
sections.translation: But what , to my sorrow , do I see afar upon the shore ? My master , the Procurer , and his Sicilian guest . both of whom wretched I supposed to have perished in the deep . Still does thus much more of evil survive for us than we had imagined . But why do I delay to run off into the Temple , and to tell

sections.label: Section 2.4.455
sections.original: Palaestrae , in aram ut confugiamus prius quam huc †
sections.translation: Palaestra this , that we may take refuge at the altar before this scoundrel of a Procurer can come hither and seize us here ? I ’ ll betake myself away from this spot ; for the necessity suddenly arises for me to do so .

sections.label: Section 2.5.458
sections.original: Pro di immortales , in aqua numquam credidi
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods , I never did imagine that there was so great delight in water ; how heartily I did draw this .

sections.label: Section 2.5.460
sections.original: nimio minus altus puteus visust quam prius .
sections.translation: The well seemed much less deep than formerly . How entirely without exertion did I draw this up . With all deference [*] to myself , am I not a very silly fellow , in having only to-day made a commencement of being in love [*] ? ( Turning slowly round , he holds out the pitcher .) Here ’ s the water for you , my pretty one ; here now , I would have you carry it with as much pleasure as I carry it , that you may please me .

sections.label: Section 2.5.465
sections.original: sed ubi tu es , delicata ? cape aquam hanc sis . ubi es ?
sections.translation: ( Stares around him .) But where are you , my tit-bit ? Do take this water , please ; where are you ? ( Again looks about .) I ’ troth , she ’ s in love with me , as I fancy ; the roguish one ’ s playing bo-peep [*] . Where are you ? Are you going now to take this pitcher ? Where are you , I say ? You ’ ve carried the joke far enough . Really , do be serious at last . Once more , are you going to take this pitcher ? Where in the world are you ?

sections.label: Section 2.5.470
sections.original: nusquam hercle equidem illam video . ludos me facit .
sections.translation: ( Looks about .) I ’ troth , I don ’ t see her anywhere , for my part ; she ’ s making fun of me . I ’ faith , I shall now set down this pitcher in the middle of the road . But yet , suppose any person should carry away from here this sacred pitcher of Venus , he would be causing me some trouble . I ’ faith , I ’ m afraid that this woman ’ s laying a trap for me ,

sections.label: Section 2.5.475
sections.original: ut comprehendar cum sacra urna Veneria .
sections.translation: that I may be caught with the sacred pitcher of Venus . In such case , with very good reason , the magistrate will be letting me die in prison , if any one shall see me holding this . For it ’ s marked with the name ; itself tells its own tale , whose property it is . Troth now , I ’ ll call that Priestess here out of doors ,

sections.label: Section 2.5.480
sections.original: ut hanc accipiat urnam . accedam huc ad fores .
sections.translation: that she may take this pitcher . I ’ ll go there to the door . ( He knocks .) Hallo there ! Ptolemocratia . ( Calling aloud .) Take this pitcher of yours , please ; some young woman , I don ’ t know who , brought it here to me . ( A pause .) It must then be carried in-doors by me . I ’ ve found myself a job , if , in fact , of my own accord , water is to be carried by me for these people as well .

sections.label: Section 2.6.485
sections.original: Qui homo sese miserum et mendicum volet ,†
sections.translation: The person that chooses himself to be wretched and a beggar , let him trust himself and his life to Neptune . For if any one has any dealings at all with him , he sends him back home equipped in this guise .

sections.label: Section 2.6.489
sections.original: edepol , Libertas , lepida es , quae numquam pedem
sections.translation: ( Surveying himself .) By my troth , Liberty , you were a clever one , who were never willing [*] to put even a foot , I ’ faith , on board ship with me .

sections.label: Section 2.6.491
sections.original: sed ubi ille meus est hospes , qui me perdidit ?
sections.translation: But ( looking round ) where ’ s this guest of mine that has proved my ruin ? Oh , see , here he comes .

sections.label: Section 2.6.492b
sections.original: Quo malum properas , Labrax ?
sections.translation: Where the plague are you hurrying to , Labrax ? For really I cannot follow you so fast .

sections.label: Section 2.6.494
sections.original: Vtinam te prius quam óculis vidissem meis ,
sections.translation: I only wish that you had perished by direful tornments in Sicily before I had looked upon you with my eyes ,

sections.label: Section 2.6.496
sections.original: quem propter hoc mihi optigit misero mali .
sections.translation: you on whose account this misfortune has befallen me .

sections.label: Section 2.6.497
sections.original: Vtinam , quom in aedis me ad te adduxisti tuas ,
sections.translation: I only wish that on the day on which you admitted me into your house , I had laid me down in a prison sooner . I pray the immortal Gods , that so long as you live ,

sections.label: Section 2.6.500
sections.original: omnes tui símiles hospites habeas tibi .
sections.translation: you may have all your guests just like your own self .

sections.label: Section 2.6.501
sections.original: Malam fortunam in aedis te adduxi meas .
sections.translation: In your person I admitted misfortune into my house . What business had I to listen to a rogue like you , or what to depart hence ? Or why to go on board ship , where I have lost even more wealth [*] than I was possessor of ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.505
sections.original: Pol minime miror , navis si fractast tibi ,
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ m far from being surprised if your ship has been wrecked , which was carrying yourself , a villain , and your property villanously acquired .

sections.label: Section 2.6.507
sections.original: Pessum dedisti me blandimentis tuis .
sections.translation: You ’ ve utterly ruined me with your wheedling speeches .

sections.label: Section 2.6.508
sections.original: Scelestiorem cenam cenavi tuam ,
sections.translation: A more accursed dinner of yours have I been dining upon than the ones that were set before Thyestes and Tereus [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.6.510
sections.original: Perii , animo male fit . contine quaeso caput .
sections.translation: I ’ m dying ; I ’ m sick at heart . Prithee , do hold up my head .

sections.label: Section 2.6.511
sections.original: Pulmoneum edepol nimis velim vomitum vomas .
sections.translation: By my troth , I could very much wish that you would vomit up your lungs .

sections.label: Section 2.6.512
sections.original: Eheu , Palaestra atque Ampelisca , ubi estis nunc ?
sections.translation: Alas ! Palaestra and Ampelisca , where are you now ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.513
sections.original: Piscibus in alto , credo , praebent pabulum .
sections.translation: Supplying food for the fishes at the bottom , I suppose .

sections.label: Section 2.6.514
sections.original: Mendicitatem mi optulisti opera tua ,
sections.translation: You have brought beggary upon me by your means ,

sections.label: Section 2.6.515
sections.original: dum tuis ausculto magnidicis mendaciis .
sections.translation: while I was listening to your bragging lies .

sections.label: Section 2.6.516
sections.original: Bonam est quod habeas gratiam merito mihi ,
sections.translation: You have reason deservedly to give me many hearty thanks , who from an insipid morsel by my agency have made you salt [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.6.518
sections.original: Quin tu hinc is a me in maxumam malam crucem ?
sections.translation: Nay , but do you get out from me to extreme and utter perdition .

sections.label: Section 2.6.519
sections.original: Eas . easque res agebam commodum .
sections.translation: You be off ; I was just going to do that very thing .

sections.label: Section 2.6.520
sections.original: Eheu , quis vivit me mortalis miserior ?
sections.translation: Alas ! what mortal being is there living more wretched than I ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.521
sections.original: Ego multo tanto miserior quam tu , Labrax .
sections.translation: I am by very far much more wretched , Labrax , than yourself .

sections.label: Section 2.6.522
sections.original: Qui ?
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.522b
sections.original: Quia ego indignus sum , tu dignus qui sies .
sections.translation: Because I am not deserving of it , whereas you are deserving .

sections.label: Section 2.6.523
sections.original: O scirpe , scirpe , laudo fortunas tuas ,
sections.translation: O bulrush , bulrush , I do praise your lot , who always maintain your credit for dryness .

sections.label: Section 2.6.525
sections.original: Equidem me ad velitationem exerceo ,
sections.translation: For my part , I ’ m exercising myself for a skirmishing fight [*] , for , from my shivering , I utter all my words in piecemeal flashes .

sections.label: Section 2.6.527
sections.original: Edepol , Neptune , es balineator frigidus :
sections.translation: By my troth , Neptune , you are a purveyor of chilly baths ; since I got away from you with my clothes , I ’ ve been freezing .

sections.label: Section 2.6.529
sections.original: Ne thermipolium quidem ullum ínstruit ,
sections.translation: No hot liquor-shop [*] at all for sure does he provide ;

sections.label: Section 2.6.530
sections.original: ita salsam praehibet potionem et frigidam .
sections.translation: so salt and cold the potions that he prepares .
